title: CrossBench: Generalized Crosstalk Benchmark Generation for Quantum Computers
abstract: As quantum computers continue to increase in size and topological complexity, benchmarking crosstalk becomes more complex and resource-intensive. This limits the ability to obtain relevant crosstalk metrics for applications such as error mitigation, quantum computer security, and circuit transpilation. There applications benefit from accessible metrics on how each gate contributes to crosstalk. However, crosstalk metrics are rarely provided by quantum computer providers and can be expensive to obtain on modern large NISQ devices. In this work, we propose CrossBench, a customizable system that generates crosstalk benchmarks for finitely large NISQ devices with arbitrary topologies. CrossBench uses a custom graph labeling algorithm to generate crosstalk benchmarks for a given quantum topology and gate set. These benchmarks can be used to estimate the average contribution of each gate's crosstalk to qubit error rates. We evaluate the effectiveness of CrossBench by generating and running benchmarks on multiple IBM quantum computers with different topologies. Our results show that CrossBench can identify gates that introduce significant crosstalk across all tested devices, with strong statistical significance (p < 0.05). These promising results show that CrossBench can give simple and accessible crosstalk benchmarks for modern NISQ systems.
summary: The paper introduces CrossBench, a scalable system for generating crosstalk benchmarks on large-scale NISQ devices. By using a graph-based approach to assign driver and spectator qubits, it provides an efficient way to identify which gates contribute most to error rates. This is crucial for improving error mitigation, circuit transpilation, and quantum hardware security.
main_problem: Traditional crosstalk benchmarking methods like Randomized Benchmarking become too resource-intensive and costly to scale as quantum computers increase in size and topological complexity.
main_result: The CrossBench system successfully identifies gates that introduce significant crosstalk across multiple IBM quantum processors with strong statistical significance (p < 0.05).
method: A customizable, scalable benchmark generator using a graph-labeling algorithm to assign 'driver' and 'spectator' qubits to detect gate-dependent error variations.
model_or_system: NISQ-era superconducting quantum processors, specifically IBM Heron-class (Fez, Kingston) and Nighthawk-class (Miami) architectures.
key_observables: The change in error rate experienced by a spectator qubit when neighboring driver qubits execute specific gates.
important_parameters: Native gate sets (X, SX, CZ, ID), driver depth (d_D), error threshold (delta), and device topology.
main_assumptions: Assumes that variations in error rates between different driver gates are primarily due to crosstalk and that identity (ID) gates introduce negligible additional error.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the circuit construction of driver and spectator qubits; Figures 2-4 are box-and-whisker plots of error rate distributions; Figures 5-7 are bar charts showing averaged CrossBench results across different IBM processors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scalability problem of crosstalk, proposes the CrossBench algorithm and graph-labeling method, details the experimental implementation on IBM hardware, presents statistical results across multiple devices, and discusses limitations and future security applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Example of spectator and driver qubits generated from spectator and driver pair (H,X). The driver generates crosstalk and the spectator attempts to observe it.
Fig. 2. Results for each driver spectator gate pair on IBM Fez. The colors represent the Driver gate, while each cluster of box and whisker charts represents a spectator gate.
Fig. 3. Results for each driver spectator gate pair on IBM Kingston. The colors represent the Driver gate, while each cluster of box and whisker charts represents a spectator gate.
Fig. 4. Results for each driver spectator gate pair on IBM Miami. The colors represent the Driver gate, while each cluster of box and whisker charts represents a spectator gate.

Figure 2 shows box-and-whisker plots of the results from all seven runs for IBM Fez. We observe a noticeable difference in error on ID and CZ spectator gates. Using a t-test, we find ID spectators experience more error with SX drivers than ID drivers (p < 0.01). We also find CZ spectators experience more error with CZ drivers than SX drivers (p = 0.021). Figure 3 shows the results for IBM Kingston. For this system, ID spectators experience higher error with X drivers than ID drivers (p < 0.01) and experience more error with CZ drivers than ID drivers (p = 0.046). Additionally, SX spectators experience more error with X drivers than with ID drivers (p < 0.01). For the Nighthawk system,...
